text: All right, welcome to another episode. Today we're talking about robotic inguinal hernia repair in patients who have previously had preperitoneal dissection, specifically with a tap inguinal hernia on the contralateral side.

This was a seventy-two-year-old man presented to my clinic with multiple hernia procedures, a symptomatic recurrent left inguinal hernia, surgical history, bilateral open inguinal hernia repairs that were tissue-based, And then a right contralateral TEP inguinal hernia repair with mesh. They, during the operation, explored the left side with no evidence of hernia. He had irreducible left inguinal hernia. Again, no mesh on that side.

But we did review their prior surgical history and operator reports found that they had used a balloon dissector during the prior repair. No significant dissection really described, acknowledged that the open repairs had probably caused some complex anatomy for an open anterior repair. And so we planned our operation.

Patient previously had an umbilical hernia repair with mesh, so we were going to definitely avoid umbilical trocar. I booked an extra thirty minutes for the case to account for potential need for license of adhesions and need for a more careful dissection of the preperitoneal plane. For this, I thought a robotic tab was still a very safe option.

The surgeon begins the procedure by developing the preperitoneal flap. An indirect hernia is visible on the left side with no mesh present. The silhouette of a mesh is observed on the contralateral side, not extending beyond the midline, which will be encountered during dissection.
The surgeon carefully performs a bloodless dissection in the preperitoneal space using heat, avoiding blunt dissection to prevent bleeding due to potential adhesions from previous repairs.

The surgeon performs lateral dissection, unaffected by previous operations, and progresses to medial dissection. The medial aspect of the contralateral mesh is visible, positioned away from the current dissection site.

The surgeon ensures visibility of the contralateral pubis to establish a landing zone for the mesh. The medial and lateral dissections have been completed, and the focus shifts to reducing the hernia sac, carefully dissecting it away from surrounding tissue.

The surgeon carefully dissects distally to bring down tissue, identifying the beginning of the hernia sac and spermatic structures. A well-demarcated edge of the sac is visible, which is reduced with ease. The focus shifts to developing a dissection to establish a clear view of the myopectenial orifice.

The surgeon carefully dissects the structures, focusing on parietalizing the vas deferens and associated vasculature. The vas deferens is clearly visible as the surgeon emphasizes creating a suitable landing zone for the mesh by alternating between easily accessible areas to develop the dissection.

The surgeon shows the previously dissected space with mesh visible on top of the bladder. A clear space is prepared for the mesh placement. Extra tissue is seen falling into the femoral canal, which is addressed by taking down the aerial tissue fibers. The external iliac artery is pointed out as it enters the femoral canal, with further development of the space.
The surgeon focuses on lateral dissection, revealing the iliopubic tract while confirming the absence of a cord lipoma.

The surgeon uses a combination of sharp and blunt dissection to develop the lateral space, carefully manipulating the spermatic cord structures and teasing the peritoneum down. The pre-peritoneal aspect of the medial umbilical ligament is taken down, allowing the vas to retract over the external iliac vein. The critical view of the myopectenial orifice is established.

The surgeon points out anatomical landmarks, drawing an imaginary line from Cooper's ligament to the lateral sidewall. An extra-large mid-weight mesh is positioned and fixated with a single stitch over Cooper's ligament.

Some people don't fixate. I try to. I think that in scenarios where you're using like a heavyweight mesh, it can be difficult. And so if, If it's very challenging to see or you have a lot of vessels over the coopers, you want to be careful.
The corner of the mesh is visible, slightly protruding from its intended position.

The surgeon adjusts the corners of the mesh to ensure proper positioning, preventing clam shelling that could lead to recurrence. A standard flap closure is performed, addressing a visible hole below the mesh. The robotic approach facilitates refined suturing techniques, allowing for efficient closure of peritoneal defects.
